    theme: cosmo

# Getting Started

## Installing and loading R packages


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
pacman::p_load(tidyverse)
```
:::


## Importing the data


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
exam_data <- read_csv("data/Exam_data.csv")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
Rows: 322 Columns: 7
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
chr (4): ID, CLASS, GENDER, RACE
dbl (3): ENGLISH, MATHS, SCIENCE

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
```


:::
:::


# Introducing ggplot

## R graphics vs ggplot

First, let us compare how R Graphics, the core graphical functions of Base R and ggplot plot a simple histogram.

::: panel-tabset
## R graphics


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
hist(exam_data$MATHS)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hand-on_Ex1_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-3-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## ggplot2


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 10,
                 boundary = 100,
                 colour = "black",
                 fill = "grey") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hand-on_Ex1_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-4-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::

:::

As shown, the code chunk is relatively simple when R Graphics is used.

# A Layered Grammar of Graphics

The elements of Grammar of Graphics are as follows.

-   **Data**: The dataset being plotted.

-   **Aesthetics** take attributes of the data and use them to influence visual characteristics, such as position, colours, size, shape, or transparency.

-   **Geometrics**: The visual elements used for our data, such as point, bar or line.

-   **Facets** split the data into subsets to create multiple variations of the same graph (paneling, multiple plots).

-   **Statistics**, statiscal transformations that summarise data (e.g. mean, confidence intervals).

-   **Coordinate systems** define the plane on which data are mapped on the graphic.

-   **Themes** modify all non-data components of a plot, such as main title, sub-title, y-aixs title, or legend background.

# Working with Data


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data=exam_data)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hand-on_Ex1_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-5-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


# Working with Aesthetics

The aesthetic mappings take attributes of the data and and use them to influence visual characteristics, such as position, colour, size, shape, or transparency. Each visual characteristic can thus encode an aspect of the data and be used to convey information.

All aesthetics of a plot are specified in the [`aes()`](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/aes.html) function call

Code chunk below adds the aesthetic element into the plot.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS))
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hand-on_Ex1_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-6-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


# Working with Geometric objects

## Plotting a Bar chart


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hand-on_Ex1_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-7-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## Plotting a Dot plot

In a dot plot, the width of a dot corresponds to the bin width (or maximum width, depending on the binning algorithm), and dots are stacked, with each dot representing one observation.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot(dotsize = 0.5)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
Bin width defaults to 1/30 of the range of the data. Pick better value with
`binwidth`.
```


:::

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hand-on_Ex1_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-8-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


The `scale_y_continuous()` is used to turn off the y-axis, and *binwidth argument* is used to change the binwidth to 2.5.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot(binwidth=2.5,         
               dotsize = 0.5) +      
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,           
                     breaks = NULL)  
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hand-on_Ex1_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-9-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## Plotting a histogram

Plot a histogram for Maths scores


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram()       
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
`stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.
```


:::

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hand-on_Ex1_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-10-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


Modify the geometric object by changing the geom() object


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20,            
                 color="black",      
                 fill="light blue")  
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hand-on_Ex1_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-11-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


Modify the geometric object by changing the aes() object


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           fill = GENDER)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 color="grey30")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hand-on_Ex1_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-12-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## Plotting the kernel estimate density plot

The code below plots the distribution of Maths scores in a kernel density estimate plot.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_density()           
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hand-on_Ex1_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-13-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


The code chunk below plots two kernel density lines by using *colour* or *fill* arguments of *aes()*


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS,colour=GENDER)) +
  geom_density()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hand-on_Ex1_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-14-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## Plotting a Boxplot


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(y= MATHS,x = GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hand-on_Ex1_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-15-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


The code chunk below plots the distribution of Maths scores by gender in notched plot instead of boxplot.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(y = MATHS,x = GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot(notch=TRUE)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hand-on_Ex1_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-16-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## Plotting a Violin Plot

The code below plot the distribution of Maths score by gender in violin plot.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(y=MATHS, x=GENDER)) + 
  geom_violin()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hand-on_Ex1_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-17-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## Plotting a Scatter Plot

The code chunk below plots a scatterplot showing the Maths and English grades of pupils.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x=MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hand-on_Ex1_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-18-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## Combining geom objects: Boxplots and Scatter plots

The code chunk below plots the data points on the boxplots by using both `geom_boxplot()` and `geom_point()`.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(y=MATHS, x=GENDER)) +
  geom_point(position="jitter", size=0.5) + 
  geom_boxplot()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hand-on_Ex1_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-19-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


# Working with stat

## Working with stat - the *stat_summary()* method

The code chunk below adds mean values by using [`stat_summary()`](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/stat_summary.html) function and overriding the default geom.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(y=MATHS, x=GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  stat_summary(geom= "point",
               fun= "mean",
               colour= "red",
               size= 4)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hand-on_Ex1_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-20-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## Working with stat - the `geom()` method

The code chunk below adding mean values by using `geom_()` function and overriding the default stat.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(y=MATHS, x=GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() + 
  geom_point(stat="summary",
             fun= "mean",
             colour= "red",
             size= 4)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hand-on_Ex1_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-21-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## Adding a best fit curve on a scatter plot

The scatterplot below shows the relationship of Maths and English grades of pupils. The interpretability of this graph can be improved by adding a best fit curve.In the code chunk below, [`geom_smooth()`](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/geom_smooth.html) is used to plot a best fit curve on the scatterplot.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(y= ENGLISH, x= MATHS)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(linewidth=0.5)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
`geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'
```


:::

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hand-on_Ex1_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-22-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


The default smoothing method is loess. The default smoothing method can be overridden as shown below.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data= exam_data,
       aes(y= ENGLISH, x= MATHS)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method= lm,
              linewidth= 0.5)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
```


:::

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hand-on_Ex1_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-23-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


# Working with facets

## Working with `facet_wrap()`

[`facet_wrap`](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/facet_wrap.html) wraps a 1d sequence of panels into 2d. This is generally a better use of screen space than facet_grid because most displays are roughly rectangular. The code chunk below plots a trellis plot using `facet-wrap()`.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 25) +
  facet_wrap(~ CLASS)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hand-on_Ex1_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-24-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## Working with facet_grid()

[`facet_grid()`](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/facet_grid.html) forms a matrix of panels defined by row and column facetting variables. It is most useful when you have two discrete variables, and all combinations of the variables exist in the data. The code chunk below plots a trellis plot using `facet_grid()`.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS)) + 
  geom_histogram(bins = 25) + 
  facet_grid(~ CLASS)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hand-on_Ex1_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-25-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


# Working with Coordinate

By the default, the bar chart of ggplot2 is in vertical form.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hand-on_Ex1_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-26-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


The code chunk below flips the horizontal bar chart into vertical bar chart by using `coord_flip()`.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = RACE)) + 
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hand-on_Ex1_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-27-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## Changing the x and y-axis range

The scatter plot below is slightly misleading because the y-axis and x-axis range are not equal.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS, y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = lm, linewidth=0.5)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
```


:::

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hand-on_Ex1_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-28-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


The code chunk below fixed both the y-axis and x-axis range from 0-100.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS, y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = lm, linewidth=0.5) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100),
                  ylim = c(0, 100))
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
```


:::

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hand-on_Ex1_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-29-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


# Working with themes

Themes control elements of the graph not related to the data. For example:

-   background colour
-   size of fonts
-   gridlines
-   colour of labels

The code chunk below plot a horizontal bar chart using `theme_gray()`.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data= exam_data,
       aes(x = RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_gray()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hand-on_Ex1_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-30-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


A horizontal bar chart plotted using `theme_classic()`.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = RACE)) +
  geom_bar() + 
  coord_flip() + 
  theme_classic()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hand-on_Ex1_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-31-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


A horizontal bar chart plotted using `theme_minimal()`.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_minimal()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hand-on_Ex1_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-32-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


# References

-   Hadley Wickham (2023) [**ggplot2: Elegant Graphics for Data Analysis**](https://ggplot2-book.org/). Online 3rd edition.
-   Winston Chang (2013) [**R Graphics Cookbook 2nd edition**](https://r-graphics.org/). Online version.
-   Healy, Kieran (2019) [**Data Visualization: A practical introduction**](https://socviz.co/). Online version
